---
title: "Spatial and Visual Navigation in Online Learning"
author:
  - name: Guillaume Gilles
abstract: |
  Our brains evolved to navigate physical spaces — and that spatial wiring
  shapes how we read and remember. When we move content to scrollable web pages,
  we strip away most of the spatial cues that help information stick. This post
  examines why that matters and what good online design can do about it.
date: 2026-04-19
categories: [Cognitive Science, Learning, Web Design]
draft: true
---

Walk into a library you know well, and you can picture — roughly — *where* the
book you need lives: third row, mid-height, to the left of the window. That
mental map was built without conscious effort. It is spatial memory at work,
and it is one of the most powerful tools your brain has for recovering
information.

Online, that map rarely forms. You scroll, you click, you lose your place —
and when you try to remember what you read, the content feels thin, like a
story heard on the radio rather than one you lived through. The problem is not
attention; it is *geometry*. The screen has been stripped of the spatial
scaffolding that cognition relies on.

## Spatial Memory: Why Location Is a Retrieval Cue

The hippocampus, the brain region most associated with spatial navigation, is
also central to the formation of *episodic* memories — memories of events
placed in time and space. The two systems are not parallel; they are deeply
intertwined. When you encode information, you automatically tag it with
contextual metadata: where you were sitting, what page you were on, roughly
how far through a document you had read.

Those tags become *retrieval cues*. "I remember it was near the bottom of a
right-hand page, close to a diagram." That feeling of geometric déjà vu is not
peripheral; it is part of the memory itself. Research on *embodied cognition*
suggests that even the physical gestures we make while reading — flipping pages,
dog-earing corners, annotating margins — contribute to recall, because they
weave motor and spatial signals into the memory trace.

::: {.callout-note}
## The *loci* method

The "method of loci" — placing items to memorise along a mentally walked route
through a familiar building — is perhaps the oldest documented memory technique,
described by Cicero. It works precisely *because* spatial memory is so durable.
We are, in a sense, trying to exploit that same hardware when we talk about
navigable online documents.
:::

## What Digital Scrolling Destroys

A physical book is a spatial object. It has thickness you can feel, a left-hand
side and a right-hand side, a rough sense of how far in you are. Even a PDF
preserves some of this: pages are discrete, numbered, fixed in position.

A long web page with infinite or near-infinite scroll has almost none of it:

- **No fixed landmarks.** The page reflows with viewport size. The paragraph
  you bookmarked visually sits in a different spot on every device.
- **No edges.** Without a clear beginning and end, the reader has no sense of
  *volume*, and therefore no sense of progress.
- **Continuous motion.** Scrolling is a kinetic act — the content moves past
  the reader rather than the reader moving through content. The frame of
  reference is inverted, and that inversion subtly impedes the formation of a
  stable spatial model.
- **No seams.** In a physical text, chapter breaks and page turns are *seams*:
  brief pauses that signal a boundary and give the brain a moment to consolidate.
  A seamless scroll eliminates them.

Andy Matuschak, whose work on [tools for thought](https://andymatuschak.org/)
has shaped how many researchers think about reading and memory, notes that most
online reading is *transient* — consumed once, poorly encoded, and quickly
forgotten. His mnemonic medium embeds spaced-repetition questions directly into
the text, forcing active retrieval rather than passive reading. The spatial
problem and the retention problem are connected: passive scrolling through
unanchored content is a recipe for shallow encoding.

Michael Nielsen makes a complementary point in
[*Augmenting Long-term Memory*](http://augmentingcognition.com/ltm.html): the
speed and ease of reading online encourages the illusion of understanding.
Reading fast feels like learning. It rarely is, unless the reader is actively
doing something with the material — stopping, questioning, testing, connecting.
Good spatial design can serve as an external prompt for that active engagement.

## Design Patterns That Preserve Spatial Awareness

The good news is that web design has developed a rich vocabulary for
re-introducing spatial cues. None of these is a silver bullet, but together
they reconstruct something of the cognitive environment that print provides.

### 1. Visual Hierarchy as a Skeleton

Headers, subheaders, pull quotes, and section breaks are not just aesthetic —
they are *landmarks*. A reader who loses their place can scan for the last
recognisable landmark and re-orient quickly. A wall of text has no landmarks
at all.

Typography reinforces this: varied heading levels, generous white space between
sections, and consistent visual rhythm all help the reader build a mental model
of the document's structure before they have read a word.

### 2. Progress Indicators

A reading-progress bar at the top of the page is a small thing that does
disproportionate cognitive work. It gives the reader a continuous answer to
"how far in am I?" — re-introducing the sense of volume that scroll destroys.
Chapter navigation sidebars (like the one Quarto generates for long documents)
serve a similar function: a persistent map of where this section sits in the
whole.

### 3. Pagination over Infinite Scroll — Where It Matters

Infinite scroll is well-suited to browsing feeds, where the reader has no
expectation of finishing. It is poorly suited to long-form documents where the
reader *should* be building a coherent model. Breaking long content into pages
or clearly delimited sections — with visible "next" and "previous" controls —
restores edges to the document and supports a sense of location within it.

### 4. Consistent Layout and Visual Anchors

If a sidebar, table of contents, or progress indicator is present, it should
stay in the same position as the reader scrolls. A sticky header tells the
reader which section they are in; a persistent table of contents lets them see,
at a glance, what they have covered and what remains. Both act as spatial anchors.

### 5. Chunking and Callouts

Dense paragraphs that run on without pause are hard to navigate. Breaking
content into shorter chunks — with callout boxes for key concepts, numbered
lists for sequences, tables for comparisons — creates visual texture that the
eye can scan. That texture also becomes a retrieval cue: "I remember it was in
a blue box near the top."

::: {.callout-tip}
## A practical test

When you finish writing a long web page, navigate away, wait five minutes, and
then try to return to a specific paragraph *without* using Ctrl+F. How quickly
can you find it? If you struggle, your readers will too — and if they struggle
to find it, they probably will not remember it either.
:::

### 6. Interleaved Retrieval Prompts

This is the strategy at the heart of Matuschak's mnemonic medium: instead of
presenting all information passively and leaving retention to chance, embed
small questions throughout the text. Even a simple "pause and recall" prompt —
"Before reading on, can you state in one sentence what you just learned?" —
shifts the reader from passive scanning to active processing, which dramatically
improves long-term retention.

## The Relationship Between Navigation and Retention

These design patterns converge on a single principle: **navigability is a proxy
for memorability**. A document that is easy to navigate spatially is a document
that the reader can build a mental model of — and a mental model is precisely
what memory needs as a scaffold.

This is why well-designed textbooks are still hard to replace: the physical
object, with its consistent layout, its tabs and bookmarks, its tactile sense
of progress, does a great deal of cognitive work that the content itself never
explicitly performs. Online, we have to do that work deliberately, through
design.

The most retentive online experiences — well-crafted documentation sites,
long-form journalism with strong visual structure, interactive tutorials — share
a family resemblance with books: they have a beginning and an end, visible
landmarks, and a coherent spatial identity. They do not try to abolish the
geometry of reading; they translate it.

## Summary

| Design element | Spatial function | Effect on retention |
|---|---|---|
| Headers & section breaks | Landmarks for re-orientation | Stronger chunking & recall anchors |
| Reading progress bar | Sense of volume and location | Reduced disorientation |
| Sticky table of contents | Persistent spatial map | Easier retrieval of location cues |
| Pagination for long content | Clear edges and seams | Better encoding of structure |
| Callout boxes & visual variety | Textural landmarks | Distinctive retrieval cues |
| Embedded retrieval prompts | Forced active processing | Deeper encoding via testing effect |

Building for spatial awareness is not about nostalgia for paper. It is about
respecting the hardware — the evolved, embodied, spatial brain — that every
reader brings to every screen.

---

## References & Further Reading

- Matuschak, A. & Nielsen, M. (2019). *How can we develop transformative tools
  for thought?* <https://numinous.productions/ttft/>

- Nielsen, M. (2018). *Augmenting Long-term Memory.*
  <http://augmentingcognition.com/ltm.html>

- Mangen, A., Walgermo, B. R., & Brønnick, K. (2013). Reading linear texts on
  paper versus computer screen: Effects on reading comprehension. *International
  Journal of Educational Research*, 58, 61–68.

- Wollscheid, S., Sjaastad, J., & Tømte, C. (2016). The impact of digital
  devices vs. pen(cil) and paper on primary school students' writing skills —
  A research review. *Computers & Education*, 95, 19–35.

- Roediger, H. L. & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). The power of testing memory: Basic
  research and implications for educational practice. *Perspectives on
  Psychological Science*, 1(3), 181–210.

- O'Keefe, J. & Nadel, L. (1978). *The Hippocampus as a Cognitive Map*.
  Oxford University Press. The foundational work connecting spatial navigation
  to memory systems in the brain.
